speaker: interviewer
text: Was the volume a little better?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry. That's a problem about drinking a lot of water. Henry, we were talking about your home life. And you said before that your mother would sing you lullabies.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there a lullaby that comes to mind? Could you share it with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, gosh. Yes. She would sing-- oh, gosh. Some Polish, but mostly Yiddish. As a matter of fact, they still-- the lullabies are still around today, or almost the same one. One that comes to mind, actually, two. One was like Pripetshik. But her favorite one was-- I remember she would sing. It's kind of a long one, but I'll try to pick it up in the middle-- in the chorus. [SINGING IN YIDDISH] I remember her voice kind of, you know, would fade away. She would smile. And sometimes we had moist eyes. That was her favorite lullaby. And songs about the [YIDDISH] who needs to get married and doesn't have [INAUDIBLE]. They're beautiful songs. You know, kind of long, sad stories about the future bride who has no dowry, you know, none. And these were the typical songs she would sing. And she would just do her work. Whether she was twisting a challah-- baking a challah or washing or whatever-- or cooking, she would just sing. No telephones, nobody to interrupt. Unless a neighbor would come in and start visiting again.

speaker: interviewee
text: So yeah. Those are fond memories of my mother. So may her soul rest in peace. What were your favorite food that your mother would make?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gosh. Every child, of course, has favorite foods and also some foods which you don't particularly like. I must say I was perhaps a picky and maybe spoiled eater, which later on in life came to haunt me. I kept thinking about that, how foolish I was. My favorite food. I always liked piquant type of food. Seasoned, spiced, you know. She would, oh gosh, she would-- and this is without cookbooks, you know. This is-- these were recipes handed down for generations. They had to do their own baking and cooking. And they want to bake or pick up a challah or have it sliced yet, you know. Or rye bread. She was a wonderful cook. My wife isn't here now. But I wouldn't-- it wouldn't be a comparison. [LAUGHS] She would bake, of course. One cake I remember she would bake was a strudel. In Yiddish it was called a [YIDDISH] strudel. It was-- I was thinking about in Polish it's [POLISH]. And in English it would be poppy seed. Poppy seed, which is grinded. And she would mix all kind of wonderful stuff in it as a filling. And then they made thin dough and dress it up a little and bake it in the oven. Oh yeah, we had gas in those days. Yeah, we had gas. Where in Czelazd it was a wood-burning stove. But we already had gas with my mother. And she knew exactly without timing, without automatic setting and set your cooking time, you know, for buzzing or shutting off the burner. All of this was done manually. And her meat dishes or dairy dishes were delicious. She knew how to roast a duck. And all the other side dishes, which were in Poland in those days. If you're familiar, they used to call [POLISH]. [INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE]. And to clean it was some kind of a job. And they would spice it and boil it. It was a delicacy. Or like a salsa, it was called [YIDDISH]. It was like Jell-O, except it was made out of-- it was a calf's legs. And they used the Jello-O type. It became like a jelly. They would slice it and eat it actually cold. It was delicious. Side dishes, you know, appetizers. And of course, having [YIDDISH] and smoked all kind of [INAUDIBLE], really wonderful appetizers. And main dishes, borscht, chicken soup, oh, wow. Chicken soup, her chicken soup I could probably patent and could market it well here. It's of course true for a lot of Jewish mothers, not only mine. Everyone would say, Mama, this chicken soup was the best.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the aroma, you know? Later on in life, I kept thinking about the aroma when you walked in the kitchen, especially on a Friday, Friday afternoon, preparing for Shabbat. The aroma of a fresh-baked Challah. And the chicken soup would, you know, I would smell it walking up the stairs in the hallway.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Gentile neighbors would always be, in a way, envious. Because she would bake and cook for Shabbat. And our Gentile neighbors would make a big deal out of baking a cake. They would spend a lot of money buying-- sometimes they would-- my dad used to say they would go and ask for advanced pay, because they got to bake for this big holiday, either Christmas or Easter, or certain the saints which they observed-- this was Catholic country-- and so they would bake big pans, you know, maybe three by four-foot pan, big cakes.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother used to bake a nice cake, a round or rectangular cake for Shabbat. And they would say, how can you bake every week? How can you have cake every week? I guess my mom knew how to budget better. So that was my mom, the cook. Any memories come to mind of Jewish holidays when you were a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Of course, the major ones-- Passover, Shavuot, and high holidays-- Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was your favorite?

speaker: interviewee
text: Passover. I like Passover. I like the story. And I like the Seder. We always had two Seders, the first and second night. And all of the melodies, which my-- chants my dad used to sing. And sometimes I chant them at our house, when I do Seder with our family and our relatives. And everybody says, let's go eat. I say, wait a minute. Listen to this chant. This is my dad's. This is one dad would chant. [HEBREW] and all that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, the amazing part about Passover that I remember-- it's kind of unique-- is, as you know, my dad was a coal miner. And he would observe Passover. And to lunch he would take his-- in his lunch bucket, he would put matzos in. And his coworkers in the mines would say, what in the world? Who didn't know him, after a while they got to know him. But any newcomers would say, what in the world are you eating? How can you work only on these crackers?

speaker: interviewee
text: But then he would pick up some matzos with eggs and-- matzah brei, except it was cold. There was no place to warm it up in the mines. But that's what he would eat. And it was sort of strange for him to pack matzos in his lunch bucket. That was my favorite holiday. The high holidays-- Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur. Shavuot, I used to like with the greenery. We used to decorate the house inside with green. I don't see it much in this country. Sukkot, unfortunately, we couldn't have a Sukkot. Because we didn't have the facilities is to build a sukkah in a tenement house with other Christians, and the courtyard was about the only place. And it wouldn't be a very conducive place to build a sukkah, with kids tearing it down, perhaps, at night. So I used to go to some friends to a sukkah, once in a while. We'd have a meal in that sukkah. High holidays, of course, were very meaningful. And I had to spend all day with my dad in Shul. And he would fast on Yom Kippur. And I remember some of the services, the chants. Rabbi Nathan Lieberman of blessed memory. We didn't have an organ or choir. But he had a wonderful voice, and he would chant some very moving chants. I remember his [HEBREW] was different. Except for the [HEBREW]. [INAUDIBLE] was about the same as we chant in this country, it was high holidays liturgy. [HEBREW] is the only liturgical selection, I found out, that doesn't have a different version of. There are perhaps hundreds of [HEBREW] and [HEBREW] and [HEBREW] and-- but not-- [HEBREW] is the only one. And it's a very, very moving chant. And I remember rabbi chanting it. He doubled as a rabbi and a cantor. He had a very good voice. And that always moved me, the chant. So those are the holidays that stand out in my mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, actually, I don't remember if had they had a name. We called it the old shul. Just the old shul. As far as, you know, name of congregations are. I became accustomed to it in this country. But other than the denominations, such as Hasidic or Conservative, very few Reform, that I remember. But as far as, you know, a name of a congregation, I don't remember too many names of congregations. We called it shuls. Or shtiebel, which is a smaller place, where Jews would meet and do the services. Called a shtiebel, meaning a room, a [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe your synagogue, once you walked inside? What did it look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Our synagogue was a huge building. [DRINKS] Excuse me. It was all-- I must tell you, first of all, our town was very limited. We only had like 80 Jewish families. So we couldn't have a great big synagogue. But later on, more Jewish people began to start moving in. So we had-- probably had a congregation of perhaps a couple hundred altogether. You know, with some newcomers.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I think, as far as the structure of the synagogue, it had steps going up. Probably, I'm going to guess, 12 to 14 steps, and a huge, stately building. The synagogue was divided. It was an Orthodox synagogue. The women were in a separate part of the synagogue, divided by like a banister type-- not banister, but trellis work. And the seats were almost like theater seats, where they would fold up.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Aron Kodesh was elevated, in the center like. And windows were stained glass windows, beautiful chandelier. Let's see what else I can remember. My father's seat, I remember, the second one from the right, with the Aron Kodesh in the center. His was on the right wing, the second one from the aisle. I remember, particularly, one incident that stands out in my mind. It was in 1938. A couple of weeks before my bar mitzvah, we had a dedication of a Torah in our shul. Some of the wealthy people-- we had some quite wealthy people in our town-- sponsored to have a scribe to transcribe a Torah. And as we dedicated it, I remember a couple of sentences from the speech that Rabbi Lieberman of blessed memory said. We were like six kilometers from the German border, which is like four miles. The nearest town was Beuthen, Germany, also a coal mining town. And the shul, the old shul in that town was burned in 1938, during Kristallnacht. And I remember Rabbi Lieberman, shortly-- it was about that time we had a dedication, a couple of weeks or three weeks later of our Torah-- and how he would compare, he made reference to-- he would say, six kilometers from here, they're burning Torahs. And we are fortunate tonight to dedicate one.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I thought-- that made a lasting impression on me. It was only within a stone's throw by today's standards. And yet, we could dedicate a Torah. And over there, they were burning. So this was a moment in my shul that I really cherish. And of course, the time of my bar mitzvah. How would you describe your bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bar mitzvah, of course, was a typical bar mitzvah. I had to get ready. I started from about, I would imagine, two years before. You're acquainted, first of all, I didn't go to Hebrew School. So we had private tutoring lesson at Rabbi Lieberman's home.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I started-- oh, on weekend, of course, I knew Hebrew. I mean, I knew how to read Hebrew. But my pronunciation was not the best. So we'd start brushing up on it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it took probably a year and a half, perhaps two years, to get prepared for the Haftarah. And the bar mitzvah was a wonderful event. I remember I did rather well with the Haftarah, because I love the chant. If you gave me a nice chant, then I would fit the words to it, you know. So everyone thought I did well. It sounds immodest, but I do remember the praises, because I chanted it well, not because I'm such a scholarly man in Hebrew. But the chant came out really well. And my folks were quite happy. Everyone got invited to kiddish and shul. Then we invited some guests to our apartment. We didn't rent a catering hall, I'm sorry to say, no band. I'm talking about the days of fountain pens, you know. I don't believe I even got a fountain pen. Certain gifts, you know, sweater, shirts, socks. a couple of books. But most important, praises and the camaraderie of the warmth of the family. And cousins and uncles came together. And they were toasting me and drink schnapps and eat herring. It's a typical old country bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have friends your age at the bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I had friends my age from-- you know, we can recall, we had a [INAUDIBLE] class. So I had friends who would attend the same class, both boys and girls. And I also had friends who we then attended meeting of like young Zionists, you know. So they all came to the bar mitzvah. And of course I was very happy to see them. And it made my bar mitzvah that much more meaningful, to be among my own.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have non-Jewish friends as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, as a matter of fact more non-Jewish friends. In view of the environment in which we lived in, my dad also had Christian friends, coal miner friends. He belonged to the union and would go to meeting places. And the solidarity among coal miners was rather strong. So yes. Taking an example from him, I had many non-Jewish friends. And I also was on a soccer team in my school. So I was rather popular. They would come to my house. We would sometimes do homework together. And my in-home professor, my brother Max, he could do-- in fifth grade, he would help other guys who were in seventh and eighth grade.

speaker: interviewee
text: So if I had any questions, and he happened to be at home, we would call him over and help us with math. Because he was super-- what he did in perhaps in 10 seconds, it would take us 15, 20 minutes to figure out. So yes, I would have them over for that purpose as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: And these were friends who I felt very close with. And I was invited to their home as well. And I would eat meals. And later on, I remember my mom would pack a meal. When I went to visit a friend, she would pack a sandwich or something, because they would feed me bread with lard. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And when my mom asked me, what did you eat there? And I told her. She says, you're not supposed to eat this! I said, but it tasted so good. So next time she would pack a little brown bag and put some cheese or some salami kosher.

speaker: interviewee
text: We would visit. And camaraderie was rather good in my childhood. I was well accepted. And of course, I spoke sort of like a street Polish and also a Silesian Polish, which is a dialect. In terms of English in the states, it would be somebody who, say from Kentucky or Tennessee or Alabama or Texas maybe, speak with someone from Chicago or San Francisco, maybe a different dialect. Except the type of Polish I spoke with my friends was not only a dialect. It was also mixed German expressions, German words. And sometimes I would Polinize them. For instance-- I can't think of anything I would say. A girl in Polish would be [POLISH]. In Silesian dialect-- not only dialect, but it was-- and in German, it's [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: So instead of saying in Polish, [POLISH], or in German [GERMAN], the Silesians made a noun out of it and said [SILESIAN POLISH]. And no one in Poland knew what [SILESIAN POLISH] is. That meant a girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, so I'm giving you a small example of how a language can be twisted and mixed. So that's the kind of Polish I spoke. Then of course, I had to learn proper Polish, when we moved. But I was one of the guys. The same as my father was one of the miners, I was one of the guys.

speaker: interviewer
text: In school you spoke proper Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, oh, yeah. I would speak-- you're so right, yes. The teacher would speak proper Polish. And he would say, what did you say? What is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [POLISH] would be [SILESIAN POLISH], taken from German. [GERMAN]. And the teacher would say, go to the blackboard. Write it out. He'd say, where did you find that word? This is not Polish. So we had to-- yes, we had to learn proper Polish. And of course, it came in handy later on. Because as I moved to Poland proper, I had to converse in that, in that Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was a means of being accepted, you know. Something the kids did. So you know, similar to what kids do today. They use of various expressions to be-- to borrow one from the kids today, I wanted to be cool. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Henry, tell me a little bit about your school.

speaker: interviewee
text: The school I-- I'm sorry to say, I only got to finish eight grades.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of school was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a public school. Actually, it was sort of a half Catholic, half public school. Because religious instructions were done by nuns and priests. And we classes till 3 o'clock, from 8 until 3. Eight grades. The curriculum was fashioned similar to what the curriculum is today, except we didn't have calculators. And I do remember abacus, however. But math we had to do by pencil. And various classes, each day, except when it came to religious instruction, religious classes-- which were like three times a week, two hours each-- either a nun would come or a young priest. However, I had the option or leaving that class entirely. I could step out. I was not bound to sit in class, so I would go out.

speaker: interviewee
text: But later in the fifth and sixth and seventh grade, I remember I stayed in. Because kids would taunt me outside and say, you know, hey, what are you doing? Are you cutting classes?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then as I would leave, you know, they would make faces, you know. So I didn't want to be different. So I sat there and listened. I wasn't given any assignment. I wasn't asked any questions. But I sat and listened. I knew-- I knew where I belonged. I knew my faith. And I knew someday I would learn my Bible lessons. But I listened. And it didn't hurt me. So I wanted to be one of them. The school itself, the relationship with other kids or teachers, was perhaps normal. Some teachers-- I remember one teacher in third grade, a lady by the name of Leipska, I even remember her name. She was not my favorite. She showed anti-Semitic tendencies. She would make references, like if there was someone to read, you know, she would say something like let the Jewish kid read now. No one ever referred to me like that, except she did. But besides her, I don't remember being any different or singled out any different from other kids. Most of the teachers were-- most of the teachers were conservative. You know, corporal punishment was also instituted. Today, you couldn't-- if I was mischievous, I used to sometimes cut up in school. And they would ask-- or she, to come forward and get 10 on my palm. Or for more severe, also on my rear.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was stringent school. But a good place to learn. And I must say, I'm grateful for having had the opportunity to go to school. Because so many-- my mom, for instance, had very little schooling. And she always insisted and made sure we don't miss a class. And she would read very slow, very poorly. So school was very important to us. How was your relationship with the other students?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very amicable relationship. Activities, of course, I would be joining in the sports activities. I was in the drama class, in the choir. I was even-- the last two years, I was even in a Christmas play. And I'm still sad-- and of course, now I understand. But I was a really hurt, because my folks wouldn't come to see me. And I had a pretty good part in the Christmas play. I think I was made up like Joseph or somebody like that. And I wanted them to come and see me. But they were against it. I would say, please, Mom, this is important to me. So they just sort of let me do it, but they didn't want to hear about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're coming to the end of reel two, Henry.
